primary_diagnosis.name: Bleeding peptic ulcer disease — acute upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage (likely Helicobacter pylori-associated) with haemodynamic compromise

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic Helicobacter pylori infection disrupts the gastroduodenal mucosal defence and drives acid-peptic injury, producing a peptic ulcer. Meal-related pain and night-time symptoms reflect acid stimulation on the ulcerated mucosa. When the ulcer erodes into a submucosal artery, brisk haemorrhage occurs: blood exposed to gastric acid becomes altered ('coffee-ground' emesis) and, passing through the gut, is digested to black tarry melaena. Ongoing loss of intravascular volume produces compensatory tachycardia and postural (orthostatic) hypotension, then presyncope, pallor, diaphoresis and delayed capillary refill — early hypovolaemic shock. Untreated, this progresses to decompensated haemorrhagic shock. Definitive control requires resuscitation (IV access, fluids/blood), acid suppression (IV proton-pump inhibitor) and urgent endoscopic haemostasis; endoscopy is also mandatory to exclude a malignant ulcer, which the associated weight loss and early satiety raise as an alarm feature.

differential.diagnosis: Bleeding peptic ulcer / upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage
differential.evidence_basis: Chronic meal-related dyspepsia now with coffee-ground emesis and melaena — classic upper GI bleed on a peptic-ulcer background.
A major cause of haematemesis; cannot be excluded remotely — needs endoscopy and assessment for chronic liver disease. Less likely without cirrhotic stigmata, but the AI cannot examine for these.
Weight loss, early satiety and chronic dyspepsia are alarm features; only endoscopy with biopsy can exclude a malignant ulcer.
A surgical emergency to be considered with severe upper abdominal pain; requires an abdominal exam (rigidity/peritonism) the AI cannot perform to exclude.
Post-vomiting mucosal tear can cause haematemesis, but does not explain the antecedent chronic ulcer pain or the melaena burden.

differential.diagnosis: Bleeding peptic ulcer / upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage with hypovolaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Coffee-ground emesis + melaena + orthostatic presyncope on a chronic ulcer history — active upper GI bleed with haemodynamic compromise.
Alarm features persist; malignancy exclusion is a mandatory endoscopic follow-up regardless of the acute bleed source.
Still cannot be excluded without endoscopy; management priority (resuscitation + urgent endoscopy + 000) is identical, so it does not change the telehealth action.

differential.diagnosis: Bleeding peptic ulcer disease — acute upper GI haemorrhage (H. pylori-associated)
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed clinically as an active upper GI bleed with haemodynamic compromise; endoscopy required to confirm the ulcer, achieve haemostasis and exclude malignancy. Diagnosis and exclusion of mimics are not achievable via telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The telehealth-correct outcome is recognition of an active upper GI bleed with haemodynamic compromise and immediate 000 escalation — NOT a definitive diagnosis. Variceal bleed, malignant ulcer and perforation cannot be excluded remotely and all demand the same emergency pathway.

red_flags.description: Coffee-ground emesis — altered blood indicating active upper gastrointestinal bleeding
red_flags.clinical_significance: Direct evidence of upper GI haemorrhage; mandates emergency assessment and endoscopy.

red_flags.description: Melaena — black tarry digested blood per rectum
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signifies a significant upper GI bleed with transit through the gut; a time-critical emergency.

red_flags.description: Orthostatic hypotension, tachycardia and presyncope — early hypovolaemic (haemorrhagic) shock
red_flags.clinical_significance: Haemodynamic compromise from blood loss; the patient reports presyncope on standing while measured orthostatic vitals are clinician-only. Predicts imminent decompensation.

red_flags.description: Unintentional weight loss (~4 kg), early satiety and chronic dyspepsia — upper GI malignancy alarm feature
red_flags.clinical_significance: Must be carried forward for urgent endoscopy with biopsy to exclude gastric malignancy; cannot be excluded remotely.

red_flags.description: Pallor, diaphoresis and delayed capillary refill (clinician-observed signs of shock)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective peripheral signs of shock; partially video-observable (pallor/sweating) but capillary refill and true perfusion status require in-person assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the presentation as simple indigestion/reflux and offering an antacid or oral PPI with routine review — missing that coffee-ground vomit and melaena signal active bleeding.
Failing to recognise that a black tarry stool is digested blood and a marker of significant upper GI haemorrhage, not a benign change.
Anchoring on benign peptic ulcer and overlooking the weight-loss/early-satiety alarm features that mandate endoscopic malignancy exclusion.

Attempting to manage at home (oral acid suppression, 'watch and wait', advising a meal or fluids) instead of escalating; the definitive care is resuscitation plus urgent endoscopy in hospital, reached only via 000.
Minting a proton-pump-inhibitor or antacid dose as an AI recommendation — a prescribing act that must come only from the pharmacology server, never from the trunk LLM.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure and orthostatic (lying/standing) vitals — BP 95/60 supine falling to 80/50 standing
Capillary refill time and true peripheral perfusion
Abdominal palpation — epigastric tenderness, and presence/absence of board-like rigidity or rebound peritonism to exclude perforation
Haemoglobin / full blood count, urea and coagulation profile
Upper GI endoscopy — the mucosal defect / bleeding vessel and malignancy exclusion
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 emergency ambulance for a suspected active upper GI bleed with haemodynamic compromise; keep the patient nil by mouth and lying flat; do not let them stand or walk; unlock the door for paramedics and stay with them.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for recognising an emergency (coffee-ground emesis + melaena + presyncope are unambiguous). The real risk is UNDER-action — mistaking it for indigestion or managing at home — and false reassurance that a specific cause (variceal bleed, malignant ulcer, perforation) has been excluded, when none can be excluded via telehealth.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG12 — suspected cancer: recognition and referral (upper GI alarm features)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Unexplained weight loss with upper abdominal pain and early satiety warrants urgent upper GI endoscopy to exclude malignancy.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Consensus guidance on acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding (e.g. NICE CG141 / Australian equivalents)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Haematemesis or melaena with haemodynamic instability requires immediate resuscitation and urgent (within 24 h) endoscopic haemostasis; pre-endoscopy IV proton-pump inhibitor and risk stratification are hospital measures.
